Glossary About
Analytic Philosophy of Mind

Ned Block

b. 1942

Access consciousness and phenomenal consciousness: a distinction that reframed the debate.

Ned Block’s work is unusually important because it sits at several turning points in late twentieth- and early twenty-first-century philosophy of mind: the fall of behaviorism, the rise and internal critique of functionalism, the re-emergence of qualia and the explanatory gap, the philosophical shaping of consciousness science, and the recent philosophy of perception/cognition interface. His view is not a single slogan such as “functionalism,” “physicalism,” “representationalism,” or “dualism.” A better reconstruction is this: Block is a naturalistic phenomenal realist who accepts the scientific study of mind, treats internal psychological organization as indispensable, rejects behaviorist and a priori functionalist reductions, distinguishes sharply between phenomenal consciousness and cognitive access, and resists reducing phenomenal character to representation, reportability, higher-order thought, or functional role.

His collected papers are explicitly organized around consciousness, functionalism, and representation, and MIT Press describes Consciousness, Function, and Representation as containing work on functionalism, phenomenal/access consciousness, neural correlates of consciousness, representationalism, and Commander Data–style questions about artificial minds; that is a fair map of the whole project.

1. Historical location: Block as both heir and critic of functionalism

Block enters philosophy of mind at the moment when behaviorism and type-identity physicalism were losing authority and functionalism was becoming the dominant alternative. The classical sequence is: logical behaviorism identifies mental states with behavioral dispositions; type-identity theory identifies mental kinds with neural kinds; functionalism identifies mental states with causal roles in a system. Block’s early work with Jerry Fodor, “What Psychological States Are Not,” belongs to the functionalist revolution because it argues against both behaviorism and type physicalism. The behavioral story is too external: what someone does depends on beliefs, desires, memories, intentions, and circumstances. The type-physicalist story is too parochial: the same psychological state could be realized in physically different organisms or artifacts.

But Block is not simply a functionalist. His famous “Troubles with Functionalism” is one of the decisive internal critiques of functionalism. It argues that functionalism inherits the very pathologies it was supposed to cure. Behaviorism is too liberal because it risks attributing mentality to things that merely behave correctly; type physicalism is too chauvinist because it risks denying mentality to possible beings with different biology. Block’s challenge is that functionalism can be too liberal or too chauvinist depending on how it is formulated. A system that realizes the relevant causal organization might still seem not to have genuine mentality or qualia; a theory defined too closely around human psychological mechanisms might exclude plausible nonhuman or alien minds.

This is why Block occupies a historically distinctive place. Putnam and Fodor helped establish multiple realizability and the autonomy of psychology from neurophysiology; Block agrees with much of that anti-reductionist motivation, but he forces functionalism to confront its limits. He helps make functionalism dominant, then helps make it philosophically unstable. The result is a mature view in which functional organization is crucial for many cognitive states, but not a complete metaphysics of mind.

2. Anti-behaviorism and the reality of inner psychological organization

Block’s anti-behaviorism is not merely the old complaint that behaviorism “leaves out inner life.” His argument is more precise: intelligent behavior is not individuated only by actual behavior, possible behavior, or behavioral dispositions. What matters is the information-processing structure that explains the behavior. In “Psychologism and Behaviorism,” Block argues that two systems could be behaviorally equivalent in actual and counterfactual respects while differing internally in ways that make one genuinely intelligent and the other not.

This yields a basic commitment of Block’s philosophy of psychology: psychological explanation is not black-box behavioral prediction. Psychology must describe internal representational, computational, perceptual, mnemonic, attentional, and inferential structures. This is why Block is a philosopher of psychology as much as a philosopher of consciousness. He thinks the explanatory units of psychology are real, but they are not reducible to overt behavior.

This also explains his interest in conceptual role semantics. In his encyclopedia article on conceptual role semantics, Block characterizes the view as one on which the meaning of a representation is its role in the cognitive life of the agent: perception, thought, decision-making, and relevant counterfactual roles are all part of the story. The significance is not that Block reduces all mentality to conceptual role. Rather, he uses conceptual role to make psychology explanatory from the inside: mental representations are not mere labels attached to behavior; they have places in a cognitive economy.

3. Functionalism: useful for psychology, inadequate as a total metaphysics

Block distinguishes between two very different projects often called “functionalism.” The first is common-sense functionalism, which tries to define mental states by the roles assigned to them in folk psychology: pain causes avoidance, beliefs combine with desires to produce action, and so on. The second is psychofunctionalism, which identifies mental states by roles in an empirically adequate psychological theory. In “Troubles with Functionalism,” he treats the latter as more scientifically serious, but also more vulnerable to chauvinism because an empirical theory built around human mechanisms may exclude differently organized minds.

The famous China-body or homunculi-headed robot case targets liberalism. Suppose a vast population of people, using radios, simulates the functional organization of a human brain and controls a body. If functionalism is true in the relevant strong sense, that system has mental states, perhaps even experiences. Block’s point is not simply “that sounds weird.” His point is that functional organization specified at the wrong level can be instantiated by systems whose mentality is deeply doubtful. Functionalism appears to license mental ascription too easily.

Yet psychofunctionalism faces the opposite danger. A creature with a radically different cognitive architecture may still believe, desire, perceive, or suffer. If a theory says it lacks those states just because it differs from us in internal mechanics, the theory is chauvinist. Block’s mature position therefore seeks an equilibrium: avoid liberalism without collapsing into biological provincialism; avoid chauvinism without identifying mentality with any abstract input-output pattern whatever.

The consequence is that Block treats functional role as indispensable but non-totalizing. It is indispensable for many explanatory categories in cognitive science. It is not sufficient for phenomenal consciousness. Functionalism may help us understand access, report, inference, decision, memory, and control. It does not, by itself, explain why there is something it is like to be in a state.

4. Multiple realizability, physicalism, and anti-reductionism

Block’s early anti-type-identity argument depends on multiple realizability: psychological kinds need not correspond one-to-one with physical kinds. A pain state, belief state, or perceptual state might be realized in mammals, octopuses, aliens, or artificial systems with different physical substrates. That undermines simple type-identity physicalism, according to which mental kind M just is neural kind N.

But Block does not infer dualism. His physicalism is cautious, naturalistic, and anti-armchair. He rejects the demand that physicalism must be secured by a priori conceptual analysis. In “Conceptual Analysis, Dualism, and the Explanatory Gap,” written with Robert Stalnaker, he argues against the idea that reductive explanation requires an a priori conceptual analysis of consciousness in physical or functional terms. The paper accepts the seriousness of the explanatory gap while resisting the move from “no conceptual reduction” to “dualism.”

This is one of the deepest tensions in Block’s thought. He is a realist about phenomenal consciousness: there are facts about experience that are not captured by behavioral, functional, cognitive, or representational redescriptions. But he is also a naturalist: he thinks consciousness has a scientific nature, even if we do not yet know how to understand it. In “The Harder Problem of Consciousness,” he explicitly distinguishes deflationism about consciousness from phenomenal realism, and he treats naturalism as a default physicalist orientation rather than as an a priori proof of physicalism.

His response to anti-physicalist arguments is therefore subtle. Against property-dualist arguments such as Max Black’s objection to mind-body identity, Block argues that some versions conflate different notions of mode of presentation: a cognitive mode of presentation, which helps fix reference or cognitive significance, and a metaphysical mode of presentation, understood as a property of the referent itself. The general strategy is: phenomenal concepts may differ radically from physical concepts without requiring phenomenal properties to be nonphysical.

5. Phenomenal consciousness and access consciousness

Block’s most famous contribution is the distinction between phenomenal consciousness and access consciousness. Phenomenal consciousness, or P-consciousness, is experience: the what-it-is-like character of seeing red, feeling pain, hearing a tone, or tasting coffee. Access consciousness, or A-consciousness, concerns availability: a content is access-conscious when it is available for reasoning, reporting, memory, and rational control of action.

The distinction matters because many theories slide between the two. A theory may explain why information is globally available, reportable, or usable in reasoning, and then claim to have explained consciousness. Block says that would at most explain access consciousness. It would not thereby explain phenomenal consciousness. In his 1995 target article “On a Confusion about a Function of Consciousness,” he calls consciousness a “mongrel” concept and argues that philosophers and scientists often conflate different notions under one word.

The distinction first appears, not under exactly the later terminology, in Block’s 1990 “Consciousness and Accessibility,” where he contrasts a notion tied to access with a notion tied to what-it-is-like experience. The 1995 paper then gives the distinction its canonical form and uses blindsight-style cases to argue that access and phenomenology can come apart. A state might be poised for use without phenomenal feel, or phenomenally present without being fully accessed, conceptualized, or reportable.

This is not a mere terminological proposal. It restructures consciousness science. Reportability becomes an epistemic tool, not a constitutive mark of consciousness. A report may tell us that a subject had an experience, but the experience is not identical to the capacity to report. Likewise, global broadcasting may explain cognitive access, but it does not automatically explain phenomenal character.

6. The explanatory gap, the Hard Problem, and Block’s “Harder Problem”

Block accepts the explanatory gap: even if we identify a neural basis of consciousness, we still face the question why that neural basis gives rise to this experience rather than another experience or none at all. But his attitude toward the gap differs from both eliminativism and dualism. He rejects deflationary views that dissolve consciousness into functional, cognitive, or representational facts, yet he also rejects the assumption that absence of conceptual reduction entails nonphysical ontology.

“The Harder Problem of Consciousness” adds another layer. The ordinary Hard Problem asks how physical processes yield experience. The Harder Problem concerns other minds and multiple realization: how can we know whether beings physically or functionally unlike us are conscious? Block’s discussion of Commander Data and related cases is meant to show that superficial functional equivalence gives defeasible evidence of consciousness, but does not settle the matter.

This is crucial for a Blockian philosophy of artificial intelligence and nonhuman minds. Block is not a crude biological chauvinist: he does not say only human brains can be conscious. But he also refuses the strong functionalist claim that the right abstract causal role is sufficient for consciousness. The realizer matters, but we do not yet know exactly how. Functional equivalence is evidence, not metaphysical guarantee.

The resulting position is epistemically uncomfortable by design. Block thinks a serious philosophy of mind must admit that consciousness is real, scientifically tractable, and yet not currently reducible to the categories that explain cognition, access, or behavior. This is the opposite of a tidy theory, but it may be the price of avoiding both deflationism and dualism.

7. Neural correlates: phenomenal NCCs versus access NCCs

Block’s P/A distinction leads directly to his work on neural correlates of consciousness. In “Two Neural Correlates of Consciousness,” he argues that we should distinguish the neural correlate of phenomenal consciousness from the neural correlate of access consciousness. The phenomenal NCC is the minimal neural basis of the content of experience; the access NCC is the neural basis of availability to consumer systems such as memory, reasoning, planning, and rational control.

This distinction changes experimental interpretation. If a brain area lights up when subjects report an experience, that activation may reflect the report, decision, working-memory retention, or cognitive access, rather than the experience itself. Block therefore warns against building report machinery into the neural basis of phenomenology. In “Consciousness, Accessibility, and the Mesh,” he argues that empirical evidence suggests phenomenal consciousness overflows cognitive accessibility and that psychology and neuroscience can “mesh” in support of that conclusion.

His later no-report work sharpens the same point. In a 2019 paper, Block argues that no-report paradigms are important because they try to separate conscious perception from post-perceptual decision and report, but he also says that no-report is not enough: subjects may still think about the stimulus. To isolate consciousness more cleanly, he argues for a “no-cognition” approach.

In his broader comparison of theories of consciousness, Block contrasts biological, global workspace, and higher-order approaches. He argues that the biological framework handles the explanatory gap better than global workspace or higher-order views, while also noting that representationist theories popular among philosophers are not the main neuroscientific contenders in that comparison. This does not mean he has a fully worked-out biological theory. It means that, for Block, phenomenal consciousness is more likely to be explained by the biological/neural basis of experience than by access, report, or metacognitive monitoring.

8. Overflow: perceptual consciousness exceeds cognitive access

Block’s overflow thesis is one of his most influential and controversial empirical-philosophical claims. He argues that perceptual consciousness can be richer than what can be cognitively accessed, reported, or stored in working memory. In “Perceptual Consciousness Overflows Cognitive Access,” he uses iconic memory and fragile visual short-term memory evidence to argue that we can consciously perceive more than we can report or think about at once.

The structure of the argument is not simply “we see a lot.” It is that experimental paradigms suggest a high-capacity phenomenal representation that exceeds the lower-capacity access mechanisms used for report. For example, in Sperling-style partial report, subjects seem to have a rich visual experience of an array even though only a subset can be accessed after cueing. Block interprets this as evidence that phenomenology outruns access.

This is a central case where Block’s philosophy and cognitive science are inseparable. The philosophical distinction between P-consciousness and A-consciousness predicts that there could be empirical cases of phenomenal overflow. The empirical work then appears, for Block, to support the philosophical distinction. That reciprocal relation between conceptual analysis and experiment is typical of his method.

The overflow thesis is also a major pressure point. Critics argue that the relevant representations may be unconscious, indeterminate, or less rich than introspection suggests. Block’s reply is not that introspection alone settles the matter, but that the best mesh of phenomenology, psychology, and neuroscience supports richness. The debate remains one of the main fault lines in contemporary consciousness studies.

9. Against higher-order theories of consciousness

Block also rejects higher-order theories, especially those that claim a mental state becomes conscious by being represented by a higher-order thought or perception. His objection is rooted in the same P/A framework: being conscious of a state is not the same as the state’s having phenomenal character. A theory of metacognitive access is not yet a theory of what-it-is-like experience.

In “The Higher Order Approach to Consciousness Is Defunct,” Block argues that the higher-order approach attempts to build consciousness from the idea that a conscious state is one the subject is conscious of, but that a serious objection to the approach remains fatal despite attempted retreats by its defenders. The details concern misrepresentation and whether a higher-order state can make a lower-order state phenomenally conscious, especially when the higher-order representation misrepresents or lacks a proper target.

The larger point is that Block consistently refuses to identify phenomenal consciousness with reflective consciousness. Infants, animals, and ordinary unreflective perceivers may have phenomenal experience without higher-order thoughts about their experiences. Conversely, metacognitive registration of a state does not by itself generate phenomenal feel.

10. Qualia, representation, and “mental paint”

Block is often classified as a defender of qualia, but the label needs care. He does not deny that experiences represent the world. He accepts that visual experiences present colors, shapes, objects, spatial relations, bodily conditions, and so on. What he denies is strong representationalism: the thesis that phenomenal character is nothing over and above representational content.

In “Mental Paint,” Block describes the opposing views as a dispute about whether phenomenal character goes beyond intentional, cognitive, or functional properties. His position is that phenomenal character does outrun representational content; he calls this “phenomenism.” He defines qualia not as spooky nonphysical items but as features of experience not philosophically reducible to nonphenomenal, cognitive, representational, or functional features. Crucially, he allows that qualia may be empirically reducible even if they are not conceptually or a priori reducible.

The “mental paint” metaphor is meant to isolate the phenomenal manner in which content is presented. An experience may represent red, but the experience’s phenomenal character is not exhausted by the proposition or content that something is red. Two experiences may differ phenomenally even if the relevant representational, conceptual, or functional descriptions fail to capture the difference.

Block’s anti-representationalism is also connected to Swampman-style and Inverted Earth arguments. In work such as “Is Experiencing Just Representing?,” he uses cases involving creatures without the right historical or external relations to challenge theories that identify experience with representation in an externalist sense. The general lesson is that phenomenal character is not fixed simply by what a state represents, at least not if representation is understood in standard externalist or teleological terms.

11. Perception, cognition, and the border between seeing and thinking

Block’s later work expands from consciousness to the architecture of perception and cognition. In The Border Between Seeing and Thinking, he argues that there is a real joint in nature between perception and cognition. Perception is constitutively iconic, nonconceptual, and nonpropositional; cognition lacks those features constitutively. He also argues that cognitive penetration occurs, that perception can include high-level properties such as faces and causation, and that perceptual categories are not concepts.

This is continuous with his earlier work. The same philosopher who distinguishes phenomenal consciousness from access consciousness now distinguishes perceptual format from cognitive format. Perceptual representation is not just belief with sensory vividness; nor is cognition just dim perception. The distinction is structural and format-based, not merely a difference in intensity.

His 2023 Behavioral and Brain Sciences article “Perception Is Iconic, Perceptual Working Memory Is Discursive” sharpens the claim: evidence that looks like perception has language-of-thought structure may actually concern perceptual working memory rather than perception itself. Perception is iconic; perceptual working memory may recode perceptual remnants in a more discursive envelope.

This is important for extracting Block’s comprehensive philosophy of mind. He is not merely defending qualia. He is defending a layered mental architecture: perception, perceptual working memory, cognition, access, and phenomenology are related but not identical. The mind is not a single representational format.

12. Mental imagery and the psychology of representation

Block’s earlier involvement in the imagery debate also belongs in the larger picture. He edited Imagery, a major collection on whether mental images are picture-like or symbol-like, and MIT Press presents the volume as addressing the “great debate” in cognitive science about the nature of mental images. His own papers “Mental Pictures and Cognitive Science” and “The Photographic Fallacy in the Debate about Mental Imagery” appear in his publication record alongside his functionalism and consciousness work.

The philosophical significance is this: Block resists crude pictures-in-the-head theories, but he also resists reducing all mental representation to language-like propositional structure. That anticipates his later distinction between iconic perception and discursive cognition. Mental imagery is therefore not an isolated topic in his work; it is part of his long concern with representational format.

His recent interest in aphantasia also fits this trajectory. His publication list includes a 2025 paper titled “Aphantasia as Imagery Blindsight,” showing that imagery remains part of his current philosophy of mind and cognitive science program.

13. The systematic philosophy of mind extractable from Block

Block’s philosophy of mind can be reconstructed as a set of theses.

First, mental taxonomy is pluralistic. There is no single essence of “the mental” captured by behavior, brain type, functional role, representation, access, or consciousness. Different mental kinds require different individuation principles. Belief, perception, pain, imagery, access, and phenomenal experience do not all belong to one explanatory category.

Second, psychology is internalist in method but not solipsistic in content. Psychological explanation must appeal to internal processing, cognitive architecture, and representational roles. Behavior alone is insufficient. But this does not imply that all content is narrow or internal; Block recognizes the force of externalist considerations while insisting that psychology needs internal explanatory kinds.

Third, functionalism is locally useful and globally false. Functional role is indispensable for explaining cognition, inference, access, and control. But functionalism as a complete metaphysics of mind is unstable. Common-sense functionalism risks liberalism; psychofunctionalism risks chauvinism; neither solves phenomenal consciousness.

Fourth, phenomenal consciousness is real. Block is a phenomenal realist: there are facts about experience that are not eliminated by explaining behavior, access, report, or representation. He rejects deflationism about consciousness, but not scientific reduction.

Fifth, access consciousness is not phenomenal consciousness. Availability for reasoning, reporting, and action is a real and important property of mental states, but it is not the same as what-it-is-like experience. Confusing these two notions produces false theories of consciousness.

Sixth, report is evidence, not essence. Reports are useful for studying consciousness, but the neural and cognitive machinery of report must not be built into the definition of consciousness. This motivates Block’s distinction between phenomenal NCCs and access NCCs, and his later insistence that no-report paradigms need to become no-cognition paradigms.

Seventh, phenomenal character is not reducible to representational content. Experiences represent the world, but phenomenal character is not exhausted by what they represent. There is “mental paint”: the qualitative manner of presentation.

Eighth, perception and cognition differ in format. Perception is iconic, nonconceptual, and nonpropositional; cognition is discursive in ways perception is not. Perceptual working memory occupies an intermediate role but should not be confused with perception itself.

Ninth, physicalism is plausible but not conceptually guaranteed. Block is friendly to naturalism and scientific explanation, but he rejects the demand that physicalism must be underwritten by a priori conceptual analysis. The gap between phenomenal and physical concepts does not automatically prove a gap between phenomenal and physical properties.

Tenth, other minds require defeasible, multi-level evidence. Functional similarity, behavior, and cognitive organization matter, but none is conclusive by itself. Consciousness may depend on realizer-level facts we do not yet understand. This is Block’s Harder Problem.

14. Block’s importance for the development of philosophy of mind

Block’s historical importance is threefold.

First, he helped move philosophy beyond behaviorism and simple identity theory. His early work with Fodor belongs to the canonical anti-behaviorist and anti-type-physicalist literature. It helped secure the thought that psychology is autonomous because mental kinds are multiply realizable and individuated at a level more abstract than neurophysiology.

Second, he exposed the instability of functionalism from within. “Troubles with Functionalism” remains central because it does not attack a strawman. It asks whether functionalism can avoid both over-attribution and under-attribution of mentality. The liberalism/chauvinism dilemma still structures debates about animal consciousness, artificial consciousness, and computational theories of mind.

Third, he reshaped consciousness studies by forcing a distinction between phenomenology and access. Many contemporary disputes about global workspace theory, higher-order theory, no-report paradigms, posterior versus prefrontal cortex, and overflow are downstream from Block’s P/A distinction. His work helped make it possible to ask whether a theory explains experience itself or only the cognitive use of information.

In that sense, Block is a bridge figure. He belongs to analytic philosophy, but his work is not insulated from psychology and neuroscience. He belongs to the functionalist era, but he is one of functionalism’s most influential critics. He is a physicalist-friendly naturalist, but he refuses to deflate consciousness into access, representation, or report.

15. Main vulnerabilities and unresolved tensions

The first vulnerability is that P-consciousness is hard to define except by examples and the “what it is like” formula. Block openly accepts that no noncircular definition may be available. This is not necessarily a defect, because many basic concepts resist reductive definition, but it leaves critics room to say that the P/A distinction depends on an unclear primitive.

The second vulnerability concerns overflow. Block’s interpretation of iconic memory and fragile visual memory is powerful but contested. The data must distinguish genuinely conscious but inaccessible contents from unconscious or merely potential contents. The philosophical thesis depends on fine empirical interpretation.

The third vulnerability is the China-body intuition. Some functionalists simply accept that a sufficiently well-organized distributed system would have mentality. Others argue that our negative intuition is unreliable because the system is too strange to imagine properly. Block’s liberalism argument is forceful, but not universally compelling.

The fourth vulnerability is the risk of biological chauvinism. Block wants to avoid saying that only human-like brains can be conscious, yet he also thinks realizer-level biology may matter. The challenge is to formulate a principled realizer-sensitive theory that does not exclude aliens, animals, or artificial systems for the wrong reasons. This is exactly why the Harder Problem is harder: it exposes a tension rather than resolving it.

The fifth vulnerability is the relation between mental paint and science. Block says qualia are not philosophically reducible to functional or representational features but may be empirically reducible. The difficulty is explaining what an empirical reduction would look like without collapsing back into the deflationary theories he rejects.

16. Reading map

For Block’s philosophy of psychology and anti-behaviorism, begin with “What Psychological States Are Not,” “Troubles with Functionalism,” and “Psychologism and Behaviorism.” These establish his critique of behaviorism, type physicalism, and functionalism, and they show why internal information-processing structure matters.

For consciousness, read “Consciousness and Accessibility,” then “On a Confusion about a Function of Consciousness,” then “Two Neural Correlates of Consciousness,” “Consciousness, Accessibility, and the Mesh,” and “Perceptual Consciousness Overflows Cognitive Access.” These form the core of the P/A distinction, the NCC distinction, and the overflow thesis.

For metaphysics and physicalism, read “Conceptual Analysis, Dualism, and the Explanatory Gap,” “The Harder Problem of Consciousness,” and “Max Black’s Objection to Mind-Body Identity.” These show Block’s position between dualism and deflationary physicalism.

For representation and qualia, read “Mental Paint” and “Is Experiencing Just Representing?” These give the anti-representationalist side of Block’s view.

For the later systematic philosophy of perception and cognition, read The Border Between Seeing and Thinking and “Perception Is Iconic, Perceptual Working Memory Is Discursive.” These extend the architecture of Block’s philosophy beyond consciousness into the structure of perception, memory, and cognition.

Final synthesis

Block’s comprehensive philosophy of mind is best understood as anti-deflationary naturalism with a pluralistic psychology of mental kinds. He thinks minds are scientifically real, internally structured, and multiply realizable. He thinks psychology must study information processing, not merely behavior. He thinks functional roles explain much of cognition but not phenomenal consciousness. He thinks phenomenal consciousness is real, but not supernatural. He thinks physicalism may be true, but not because consciousness can be analyzed away. He thinks perception, cognition, access, and experience are distinct layers of mind, and philosophy goes wrong when it collapses them.

The central Blockian warning is therefore: do not mistake one legitimate explanatory level for the whole mind. Behavior is not the whole mind. Neural type is not the whole mind. Functional role is not the whole mind. Representation is not the whole mind. Cognitive access is not the whole mind. Report is not the whole mind. The mind is a layered natural phenomenon, and consciousness is the layer that most resists reduction to the others.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
